methods.summary: Returns the current count of a specific building type in a city.
notes: Use this to test whether a city already has a building before granting yields, promotions, or dummy-building side effects.
For most standard buildings the answer is 0 or 1, but dummy-building patterns can intentionally stack counts above one.
If you only need a yes or no answer, City:IsHasBuilding is usually simpler to read.

example.summary: Use a yes or no building check before applying a city effect.
example.code:
local iBuilding = GameInfoTypes.BUILDING_MONUMENT
local pCity = UI.GetHeadSelectedCity()
if pCity and pCity:IsHasBuilding(iBuilding) then
	print("The selected city already has a Monument")
end

gotchas: The parameter is a BuildingType row ID, not a BuildingClass.
Dummy-building systems may legitimately return values greater than one.
If you care about stacked real-building counts specifically, City:GetNumRealBuilding is usually the clearer read.

relatedSchemaNotes.note: Look here first for per-building yield, specialist, and prerequisite data.
Class rows matter when unique buildings replace a shared class.

seeAlso.note: Use the construct hook when you need to block or allow a building before it enters production.

methods.summary: Returns the owning player ID for the city.
notes: Use this when a city object is already in hand and you need to branch on its current owner.
This is useful after city transfer, capture, or liberation flows where the city's owner may have changed since the surrounding logic began.
The return value is lightweight and easy to combine with Players[...] lookups or team checks.

example.summary: Resolve the owner of a city object before applying a city-scoped effect.
example.code:
local pCity = UI.GetHeadSelectedCity()
if pCity then
	local iOwner = pCity:GetOwner()
	if iOwner ~= -1 then
		print("City owner:", Players[iOwner]:GetName())
	end
end

gotchas: The return value is a player ID, not a Player object.
This reports the city's current owner, not the original founder or previous owner.

seeAlso.note: Plot ownership helper when you start from map coordinates instead of a City object.

methods.summary: Returns the religion with the most followers in the city.
notes: Use this when a city-level effect should track whichever religion currently has the most followers there.
This is a follower-count result, so it is a natural fit for majority-religion dummy buildings or conversion-sensitive city bonuses.
If you need to branch on pantheons, remember they all share the same religion ID.

example.summary: Refresh a city-only bonus when Buddhism has the most followers.
example.code:
local iReligion = city:GetReligiousMajority()
local iRequiredReligion = GameInfoTypes.RELIGION_BUDDHISM
city:SetNumRealBuilding(GameInfoTypes.BUILDING_DUMMY_PILGRIM_HOSTEL, iReligion == iRequiredReligion and 1 or 0)

gotchas: This reports the religion with the most followers, not the religion exerting the most pressure.
All pantheons share the same religion ID, so this call cannot distinguish one pantheon belief package from another by ID alone.

seeAlso.note: Hook companion when the majority change itself should trigger the follow-up effect.

methods.summary: Returns the player ID currently treated as active by the running game or UI context.
notes: In UI code this is usually the local human player, and the usual next step is Players[Game.GetActivePlayer()].
This is a stable helper for frontend and in-game UI code that needs a player handle without already carrying one around.
It is also a common bridge into player-scoped methods like notifications, production lookups, and diplomacy UI.

example.summary: Resolve the active player object before calling other player-scoped helpers.
example.code:
local iPlayer = Game.GetActivePlayer()
local pPlayer = Players[iPlayer]
if pPlayer then
	print("Active player ID:", iPlayer)
end

gotchas: Do not assume the active player is the same thing as the turn owner in every UI flow or multiplayer situation.
This returns an ID, not a Player object, so callers still need to index into Players.

seeAlso.note: Common follow-up once you have the active player object.
Do not confuse active-player UI context with the per-player turn hook.

methods.summary: Returns the current game turn index used by the running match.
notes: Use this for lightweight timing checks, cooldown comparisons, and persistence keys that need a stable turn marker.
This is a better fit than calendar strings when logic only cares about sequence and not display year formatting.
It is especially useful in recurring hooks like PlayerDoTurn when you want to avoid running the same effect twice in one turn.

example.summary: Compare the current turn to a saved marker before running an effect again.
example.code:
local iTurn = Game.GetGameTurn()
print("Current turn:", iTurn)

local lastProcessedTurn = Modding.OpenSaveData().GetValue("CMC_LastProcessedTurn")
if lastProcessedTurn ~= iTurn then
	print("This turn has not been processed yet")
end

gotchas: The turn number is not the same thing as the displayed world year.
Do not confuse a turn counter with active-player UI context; many players can still be processed during the same turn.

seeAlso.note: Natural recurring hook for turn-indexed gameplay effects.

methods.summary: Pushes a notification onto a specific player's notification stack.
notes: Use this when gameplay code needs to surface a specific event to one player without opening a full popup.
Pass -1 for x and y when the notification is not tied to a world plot.
Choose the notification type carefully because the game uses it to decide icon behavior and click handling.

example.summary: Notify the active player about something that happened at their capital.
example.code:
local iPlayer = Game.GetActivePlayer()
local pPlayer = Players[iPlayer]
local pCapital = pPlayer:GetCapitalCity()
if pCapital then
	pPlayer:AddNotification(
		NotificationTypes.NOTIFICATION_GENERIC,
		"A scripted effect resolved in your capital.",
		"Scripted Event",
		pCapital:GetX(),
		pCapital:GetY()
	)
end

gotchas: Some notification types expect extra payload data and will feel broken if iData and iDataExtra are always left at defaults.
Notification text should already be localized before it reaches this method.

seeAlso.note: Common way to resolve the local player before sending a notification.

methods.summary: Checks whether a player is currently allowed to train a unit type under the present game state.
notes: Use this before exposing custom production shortcuts or auto-queue logic that assumes the player can legally build a unit.
This is a player-wide validation, so it is a good fit for civ-wide rules, policy locks, and resource gate logic.
If you need city-specific availability checks, pair it with city production logic instead of relying on this call alone.

example.summary: Guard a UI action before trying to queue a unit for the active player.
example.code:
local iPlayer = Game.GetActivePlayer()
local pPlayer = Players[iPlayer]
local iUnit = GameInfoTypes.UNIT_ARCHER
if pPlayer and pPlayer:CanTrain(iUnit, false, true, false, false) then
	print("Active player can currently train an Archer")
end

gotchas: The boolean flags materially change the answer, so do not cargo-cult a single flag set into every caller.
A true result here does not mean a specific city can produce the unit this turn; city context may still block it.

relatedSchemaNotes.note: This hook keys directly off unit rows, so trait overrides and production rules usually start here.
Check class-level replacement relationships when a civ swaps one unit row for another.

seeAlso.note: Event veto hook for adding extra train rules.
Inspect the unit rows and prereqs behind the queried type.

local pPlayer = Players[ePlayer]

methods.summary: Tests whether a unit can airlift to the target location right now.

example.summary: Check whether the selected unit can airlift to its current plot coordinates.

local pUnit = UI.GetHeadSelectedUnit()
if pUnit then
	local pPlot = pUnit:GetPlot()
	if pPlot and pUnit:CanAirliftAt(pPlot, pPlot:GetX(), pPlot:GetY()) then
		print("Selected unit has a valid airlift target check")
	end
end

gameEvents.name: BuildFinished
gameEvents.summary: Hook fired when a build action finishes on a plot and resolves into an improvement result.
notes: Use this after a build has resolved on the map, not while a worker is still deciding whether the action is legal.
The hook gives you player and plot coordinates, which is enough to resolve the plot and inspect terrain, owner, and follow-up effects.
In practice this hook is bugged and can fire twice, so real gameplay effects should defensively dedupe themselves.

example.summary: Guard against the duplicate callback before rewarding a finished Farm.
example.code:
local save = Modding.OpenSaveData()

GameEvents.BuildFinished.Add(function(iPlayer, iPlotX, iPlotY, eImprovement)
	local sKey = string.format("BuildFinished_%d_%d_%d_%d_%d", Game.GetGameTurn(), iPlayer, iPlotX, iPlotY, eImprovement)
	if save:GetValue(sKey) then
		return
	end
	save:SetValue(sKey, 1)

	if eImprovement == GameInfoTypes.IMPROVEMENT_FARM then
		Players[iPlayer]:ChangeGoldenAgeProgressMeter(25)
	end
end)

gotchas: This hook is bugged and commonly fires twice for the same resolved build.
Builds without an associated improvement row, such as roads, route removal, improvement removal, or terrain clearing, all report eImprovement as -1 here.
The improvement parameter reflects the completed improvement row, not necessarily the original build action enum chosen by the unit.
If you need to reason about the worker action itself, you will often need to cross-check Builds as well as Improvements.

relatedSchemaNotes.note: Use the completed improvement row for yield changes, pillage rules, and upgrade behavior.
The worker action that produced the result is documented separately from the finished improvement row.

seeAlso.label: Builds
seeAlso.note: Cross-check build actions when the finished improvement alone is not enough context.
Upstream mission validation before the worker build resolves into a finished improvement.
Resolve the completed plot state once the improvement has actually landed on the map.

seeAlso.label: Build Finished Trigger

seeAlso.label: Map Viewer
seeAlso.note: Use the map tools when the finished build logic needs terrain, feature, resource, or plotted follow-up debugging.

gameEvents.name: CanDeclareWar
gameEvents.summary: Team-level veto hook for whether one team can declare war on another team.
notes: Use this when diplomacy restrictions should apply before war is declared, not after the fact.
Because the hook works on team IDs, it is suitable for broad scenario rules and alliance-based restrictions.
This is a clean place to stop early-game wars, permanent peace rules, or scripted diplomacy locks.

example.summary: Block declarations of war before a chosen turn threshold.
example.code:
GameEvents.CanDeclareWar.Add(function(eFromTeam, eToTeam)
	if Game.GetGameTurn() < 50 then
		return false
	end
	return true
end)

gotchas: The hook uses team IDs, not player IDs.
A blanket false return can freeze diplomacy across the entire match if the handler is not narrowly scoped.

seeAlso.note: Simple timing helper for scenario-style war locks.
Useful when war permissions depend on tech ownership at the team level.
Method validation for the same rule path.

gameEvents.name: CanHavePromotion
gameEvents.summary: Unit-level veto hook for deciding whether a specific unit can receive a promotion.
notes: Use this to enforce custom promotion rules that depend on scenario logic, civ design, or special unit states.
The hook runs before the promotion is accepted, so it is a clean place to reject invalid promotion paths.
Because it only receives a unit ID, promotion gating logic should resolve the unit object explicitly when deeper inspection is needed.

example.summary: Prevent one promotion from being granted through the normal path.
example.code:
GameEvents.CanHavePromotion.Add(function(iPlayer, iUnit, ePromotion)
	if ePromotion == GameInfoTypes.PROMOTION_MARCH then
		return false
	end
	return true
end)

gotchas: Returning false is a veto, so broad handlers can accidentally suppress large parts of the promotion tree.
The unit argument is a unit ID, not a Unit object.

relatedSchemaNotes.note: Promotion rows define the actual promotion being filtered by the hook.

seeAlso.note: Related unit gating hook for upgrade paths rather than promotion ownership.

gameEvents.name: CanHaveUpgrade
gameEvents.summary: Unit-level veto hook for whether a specific unit may upgrade into a target unit type and class.
notes: Use this to enforce custom upgrade restrictions that depend on civ rules, scenarios, or temporary gameplay states.
The hook provides both the unit class and the target unit row, which makes it useful for unique unit replacement logic.
This is one of the cleaner places to stop an upgrade path before it appears to the player as valid.

example.summary: Disallow a specific upgrade target through the event layer.
example.code:
GameEvents.CanHaveUpgrade.Add(function(iPlayer, iUnit, eUnitClass, eUnit)
	if eUnit == GameInfoTypes.UNIT_MACHINE_GUN then
		return false
	end
	return true
end)

gotchas: The unit parameter is a unit ID, not a Unit object.
Returning false blocks the upgrade path entirely, so checks should stay tightly targeted.

relatedSchemaNotes.note: Target unit rows define what the upgrade is trying to become.
The class parameter helps when civ uniques remap the same class to different units.

GameEvents.CityBoughtPlot.Add(function(iPlayer, iCity, iPlotX, iPlotY, bGold, bFaithOrCulture)

gameEvents.name: CityCanConstruct
gameEvents.summary: City-level hook for permitting or vetoing whether one specific city can construct a building type.
notes: Use this when the rule depends on city-local state such as terrain, local dummy buildings, conquest status, or nearby resources.
The city parameter is a city ID, so callers usually resolve the city through the owning player's city collection before inspecting it.

example.summary: Hide a building unless the target city already has a prerequisite dummy building.
example.code:
GameEvents.CityCanConstruct.Add(function(iPlayer, iCity, eBuilding)
	local pPlayer = Players[iPlayer]
	local pCity = pPlayer and pPlayer:GetCityByID(iCity)
	if pCity and eBuilding == GameInfoTypes.BUILDING_GARDEN then
		return pCity:GetNumBuilding(GameInfoTypes.BUILDING_MONUMENT) > 0
	end
	return true
end)

notes: Use this for ally-threshold rewards, influence tracking, or diplomatic logic that only cares about the ally transition.

example.summary: Apply a major-civ bonus after an ally-state change with a city-state.
example.code:
GameEvents.MinorAlliesChanged.Add(function(eCS, ePlayer, bGainedLost, iOldValue, iNewValue)
	local pPlayer = Players[ePlayer]
	local pCapital = pPlayer and pPlayer:GetCapitalCity()

		pCapital:SetNumRealBuilding(GameInfoTypes.BUILDING_DUMMY_ALLY_NETWORK, pPlayer:GetNumMinorCivsAllied() > 0 and 1 or 0)
	end
end)

gameEvents.name: MinorFriendsChanged

GameEvents.MinorFriendsChanged.Add(function(eCS, ePlayer, bGainedLost, iOldValue, iNewValue)
	local pPlayer = Players[ePlayer]

			pCity:SetNumRealBuilding(GameInfoTypes.BUILDING_DUMMY_PATRONAGE_TRADE, pPlayer:GetNumMinorCivsFriends() >= 3 and 1 or 0)
		end

end)

gameEvents.name: PlayerCanConstruct
gameEvents.summary: Player-level hook for permitting or vetoing access to a building type across the player's empire.
notes: Use this for civ-wide or empire-wide building rules that should apply before a specific city gets involved.

	local pPlayer = Players[iPlayer]
	if pPlayer and eBuilding == GameInfoTypes.BUILDING_GARDEN then
		return pPlayer:IsPolicyBranchFinished(GameInfoTypes.POLICY_BRANCH_TRADITION)
	end
	return true
end)

gotchas: This hook does not tell you which city is evaluating the building, so city-local restrictions belong elsewhere.
The building parameter is a BuildingType row ID, not a BuildingClass.

gameEvents.name: PlayerCanTrain
gameEvents.summary: Player-level hook for vetoing or permitting access to a unit type before production proceeds.
notes: Use this to inject extra civ-wide train rules without rewriting every city production surface yourself.
Keep handlers narrow and return quickly for players you do not intend to modify.
This hook is best for broad player state checks such as traits, eras, game options, or scenario rules.

example.summary: Block a specific unit type for minor civs.
example.code:
GameEvents.PlayerCanTrain.Add(function(iPlayer, eUnit)
	local pPlayer = Players[iPlayer]
	if pPlayer and pPlayer:IsMinorCiv() and eUnit == GameInfoTypes.UNIT_ARCHER then
		return false
	end
	return true
end)

gotchas: Treat false as a veto and avoid broad handlers that accidentally suppress unrelated unit types.
This hook does not tell you which city is asking, so it is the wrong place for local building or terrain requirements.

relatedSchemaNotes.note: This is the main row set for unit costs, prereqs, combat classes, and upgrade chains.

gameEvents.name: PlayerDoTurn
gameEvents.summary: Per-player turn hook that fires during the turn cycle rather than only for the active human UI context.
notes: This is a strong default hook for per-player upkeep, counters, deferred maintenance, and scenario bookkeeping.
Modder experimentation on the wiki reports that it fires at the start of turn rather than after player actions resolve.
Use the passed player ID directly instead of assuming the active player matches the turn currently being processed.

example.summary: Run a once-per-turn maintenance check for every alive player.
example.code:
GameEvents.PlayerDoTurn.Add(function(iPlayer)
	local pPlayer = Players[iPlayer]
	if pPlayer and pPlayer:IsAlive() then
		print("Processing scripted upkeep for player", iPlayer)
	end
end)

gotchas: The wiki notes that this does not appear to run on the first turn of the game, only for the human player on turn two, and for all players after that.
Because it runs for every player, expensive logic here can scale poorly in large games.

seeAlso.note: Useful contrast when you need local UI context instead of the current turn owner.

example.code:
GameEvents.ReligionFounded.Add(function(iPlayer, iCity, eReligion, eBelief1, eBelief2, eBelief3, eBelief4, eBelief5)
	local pPlayer = Players[iPlayer]

	if pCity then

	end
end)

gameEvents.name: UnitPrekill
gameEvents.displaySignature: GameEvents.UnitPrekill(PlayerTypes:ePlayer, int:iUnit, UnitTypes:eUnit, int:iPlotX, int:iPlotY, bool:bDelay, PlayerTypes:eByPlayer)
gameEvents.summary: Hook fired just before a unit is removed from the game.
notes: Use this for death rewards, cleanup, or final state capture that must happen before the unit disappears.
UnitPrekill fires twice in practice, first with bDelay true and then again with bDelay false.
If you need live unit data, do the work on the bDelay true call before the unit is cleared from the game.

example.summary: Capture the dying unit's location on the first callback only.
example.code:
GameEvents.UnitPrekill.Add(function(iPlayer, iUnit, eUnit, iPlotX, iPlotY, bDelay, eByPlayer)
	if not bDelay then
		return
	end

	local pPlayer = Players[iPlayer]
	local pUnit = pPlayer and pPlayer:GetUnitByID(iUnit)
	if pUnit then
		print("Unit removed at", iPlotX, iPlotY, pUnit:GetName())
	end
end)

gotchas: On the second callback the dying unit has already been cleared out, so resolving it by unit ID will usually fail.
eByPlayer is unreliable here and is commonly -1.
Melee killers may already have moved onto the tile by the time you inspect the plot, so test location-sensitive logic carefully.

seeAlso.note: Resolve the unit object before delayed removal clears it from the player roster.
